result.content: FINAL_ANSWER: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, follow this algorithmic approach: 1. Data Representation: Represent the final tree using an adjacency list where each node points to its children. Maintain a mapping or property for each node in the final tree that identifies which original nodes from the initial tree were merged to form it. 2. Algorithm (Post-order Traversal): Perform a post-order traversal (bottom-up) on the final tree. This ensures that when we process a node, all its descendants (which may themselves be results of merges) have already been processed. For each node u in the final tree: a. Identify its children c1, c2, ..., ck in the final tree. b. These children ci represent the groups of original siblings that were merged under u. c. For each child ci, if it represents a group of m original siblings {s1, s2, ..., sm}, generate m-1 merge operations. A standard way to record this is to merge them sequentially: (s1, s2), (s1_union_2, s3), ..., (s1_to_m-1, sm). d. Note that the result of these merges is the node ci itself in the context of the final tree structure. 3. Implementation Details: Use a stack to store the sequence of merge operations as they are discovered during the traversal. To handle the N=10^5 constraint, ensure the traversal is iterative or uses an increased recursion limit to avoid stack overflow. The complexity will be O(N) because each node and each merge operation is visited/processed a constant number of times. JUSTIFICATION: The post-order traversal ensures that we reconstruct the merges from the leaves up to the root, respecting the hierarchical structure of the tree. By treating each child of a node in the final tree as a set of original siblings that were merged, we can systematically output the required sequence of operations in linear time.
